fact: July 12 is exactly 193 days into the year, which means there are only 172 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak summer heat season - the sun stays up for over 15 hours in many places, making it perfect for epic water balloon fights!
Cicadas are typically screaming their loudest around this time of year in North America, creating a buzzing symphony that can reach 120 decibels - louder than a chainsaw!
July 12 falls in Cancer season, when the moon's pull is strong and tides can be surprisingly high.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, this date marks deep winter - penguins are huddling together for warmth while kids up north are swimming!
Fireflies often reach their peak blinking power around this time in temperate regions, flashing up to 6 times per minute to find mates in the warm summer darkness.
The Earth is actually moving slower in its orbit around this date - we're near our farthest point from the sun, called aphelion, even though it feels blazing hot!
Sunflowers are typically turning their giant heads to follow the sun's path across the sky, doing a slow-motion dance that takes all day to complete.
In northern regions like Alaska, the sun barely sets around July 12, creating an eerie twilight that lasts almost all night long.
This is usually prime thunderstorm season in many temperate areas - the hot ground creates rising air that builds towering storm clouds reaching 40,000 feet high!
Mosquitoes are often at their most annoying around this date in humid climates because the warm air makes them super active and hungry for blood.
July 12 sits right in the middle of summer's hottest stretch in the Northern Hemisphere, when asphalt gets so scorching it can actually cook an egg in some desert areas!
